slides.title: Life in the Wild
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 6 English, Unit 3: Life in the Wild. Welcome to Unit 3: Life in the Wild! In this unit, you will explore the natural world through the lens of East African ecosystems. You will discover how animals, plants, and their environments are connected in amazing ways. Every living thing depends on something else to survive, and understanding these connections helps us take better care of the world around us. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Describe how food chains and ecosystems work, using the correct scientific vocabulary
Use the present simple tense to state facts about animals and nature
Build complex sentences using relative clauses with who, which, and where
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to describe how food chains and ecosystems work, using the correct scientific vocabulary. you will also use the present simple tense to state facts about animals and nature. you will also build complex sentences using relative clauses with who, which, and where. Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: threat, treacherous, dictator, oppress, hardship, persecute. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
bullets: Life in the Wild: source text 1
Life in the Wild: source text 2
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes Life in the Wild: source text 1 and Life in the Wild: source text 2. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: The Present Simple Tense — Stating Facts
bullets: What it means and why it matters The present simple tense is used to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always or generally true. When we write...
slides.narration: The first language focus is The Present Simple Tense — Stating Facts. What it means and why it matters The present simple tense is used to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always or generally true. When we write about science, nature, or the world around us, we use this tense to state facts. For example, 'The Earth orbits the Sun' is a fact that is always true, so we use the present simple. Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Vocabulary Expert Interview. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
